00:47:10	Francesco D'Amato:	Few questions:
Why do this now, when everything else is still quantum vulnerable?
Are we even sure that we’ll be using RANDAO in 3-4 years?
Even if so, why don’t we wait until the validator set is much smaller?
00:47:35	Gottfried Herold:	The stored state changes with each usage, so this does not lend itself to reuse easily.
00:47:54	Yann Vonlanthen:	Reacted to "The original hash on..." with 👀

Even if so, why don’t we wait until the validator set is much smaller?" with 👍
00:50:05	Francesco D'Amato:	Another point is we might not even use committees in the future (though a shuffle could still be useful for the proposer selection)
00:50:07	Gottfried Herold:	Potuz: rotating BLS keys with very small lifetime don't quite work for the RANDAO usage.
00:50:30	Etan (Nimbus):	historically, iterative worked better
00:50:39	lightclient:	Replying to "Potuz: rotating BLS keys with very small lifetime don't quite work for the RANDAO usage."

why is that?
00:50:42	Matthew Keil:	Iterative feels much better
00:50:45	potuz:	I disagree with Kev on that statement, but @Gottfried Herold rotating per finalized epoch does
00:50:49	Justin Traglia:	Reacted to "historically, iterat..." with 👍
00:50:51	Justin Traglia:	Reacted to "Iterative feels much..." with 👍
00:50:58	lightclient:	Reacted to "historically, iterative worked better" with 👍
00:51:02	Gottfried Herold:	(because the users can choose the pubkey; you would need to have users choose pubkey with a delay, which is not good for the PQ use case)
00:51:10	Dustin:	Reacted to "Iterative feels mu..." with 👍
00:51:12	Dustin:	Reacted to "historically, iter..." with 👍
00:51:18	Nico Flaig:	Reacted to "Iterative feels much..." with 👍
00:51:21	Nico Flaig:	Reacted to "historically, iterat..." with 👍
00:51:29	potuz:	@Gottfried Herold the point is that rotating is deterministic, the users cannot choose the key
00:51:44	Gottfried Herold:	If deterministic, then it would work, yes.
00:51:45	potuz:	That’s my proposal, the deterministic derivation depends on a committed PQ safe key
00:51:49	Gottfried Herold:	Ah, OK.
00:51:54	potuz:	Yes, this approach is my favorite,
00:52:00	potuz:	That’s why I believe all this is premature
00:53:17	Francesco D'Amato:	Generally agree with the iterative approach, but I agree it feels premature
00:53:32	potuz:	Iterative makes sense once the final approach is established
00:53:45	cayman:	Reacted to "Iterative makes sens..." with 👍
00:53:54	potuz:	It doesn’t make sense to put us on a path enshrining the first iteration when the final approach is not decided
00:54:13	Nico Flaig:	Reacted to "Iterative makes sens..." with 👍
00:54:13	Etan (Nimbus):	Reacted to "It doesn’t make sense to put us on a path enshrining the first iteration when the final approach is not decided" with 👍
00:54:25	Dustin:	Reacted to "It doesn’t make s..." with 👍
00:54:26	Dustin:	Reacted to "Iterative makes se..." with 👍
00:54:32	Etan (Nimbus):	Replying to "It doesn’t make sens..."

still, having the EIP as a documentation of the current research makes sense, even if it doesn't go beyond H* PFI initially
00:54:41	potuz:	Reacted to "still, having the EIP as a documentation of the current research makes sense, even if it doesn't go beyond H* PFI initially" with 👍
00:55:02	Justin Traglia:	Reacted to "Iterative makes sens..." with 👍
00:56:33	Gottfried Herold:	I mean, an alternative (just for RANDAO) would be signing the epoch number (like we do today) with a (static) pubkey of a determinstic signature scheme and authenticate the static pubkey with a fixed slote of the PQ signature scheme. The latter is a one-time scheme, but that is fine because you always authenticate the same static pubkey. This avoids adding data to state...
00:57:33	Francesco Risitano (Tau):	Replying to "There's a sort of de..."

I believe the execution payload already contains the block hash and the engine api validates it. I suppose the main change here is that in the time between the bid and the payload reveal the state root and block hash are unknown - instead there will be some other partial commitment digest that could be used.
00:57:36	Matthew Keil:	exactly
00:57:52	Francesco D'Amato:	Reacted to "still, having the EI..." with 👍
00:57:59	potuz:	Replying to "I mean, an alternati..."

Yes this is precisely what I want to do but even for attestations so that we keep attestations being aggregatable by everyone
00:58:16	potuz:	Reacted to "I believe the execution payload already contains the block hash and the engine api validates it. I suppose the main change here is that in the time between the bid and the payload reveal the state root and block hash are unknown - instead there will be some other partial commitment digest that could be used." with 👍
00:58:20	Etan (Nimbus):	xmss is stateful, signing requires writing to flash, no?
00:58:56	Saulius Grigaitis | Grandine:	IMO It would be better to have all the PQ schemes finalised (including particular hash function etc) before including anything to the protocol.
00:59:32	potuz:	Replying to "There's a sort of de..."

Yes, I think this is the cleanest that we validate it on new payload, but the problem is that implementations do use the has committed and not necessarily revealed
00:59:54	potuz:	Reacted to "IMO It would be better to have all the PQ schemes finalised (including particular hash function etc) before including anything to the protocol." with 👍
00:59:55	Gottfried Herold:	Replying to "I mean, an alternati..."

I mean, for the RANDAO usecase, we don't even have aggregatibility or efficiency as really critical requirements.
01:00:00	Luca Donno | L2BEAT:	Reacted to "It doesn’t make sense to put us on a path enshrining the first iteration when the final approach is not decided" with 👍
01:00:03	Luca Donno | L2BEAT:	Reacted to "still, having the EIP as a documentation of the current research makes sense, even if it doesn't go beyond H* PFI initially" with 👍
01:00:35	potuz:	Replying to "I mean, an alternati..."

Sure, but the same system described above: deterministically generating a BLS pair for example, would work for both
01:01:56	Gottfried Herold:	Replying to "I mean, an alternati..."

I agree. *If* we go for the BLS route, this seems the right thing. But the approach works with a non-BLS scheme even if we don't.
01:01:59	Parithosh Jayanthi:	https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-8015
01:03:10	Francesco Risitano (Tau):	I see yes, in practice I guess this sort of introduces two “primary keys” / identifiers for a single payload which does add some complexity. My main motivation behind suggesting this change is that I think the 7862 delayed state root proposal is quite invasive and impacts state proof latency which could have practical implications for cross chain messages and light clients. I think the net complexity of this solution is lower. In regards to the prover stack the post state root contributes approximately ~10% of the proving workload - I don’t think the 7862 complexity justifies a 10% proving hot path reduction imo.
01:03:23	Justin Traglia:	Technically, would this reduce state growth? 😄
01:04:10	Etan (Nimbus):	Replying to "Technically, would t..."

it reduces state size, it doesn't slow down future growth
01:04:20	Justin Traglia:	Replying to "Technically, would t..."

So yes, technically lol
01:04:29	Greg the Egg:	https://github.com/ethereum/pm/issues/2157#issuecomment-4981597977
01:04:38	Francesco Risitano (Tau):	Reacted to "Yes, I think this is the cleanest that we validate it on new payload, but the problem is that implementations do use the has committed and not necessarily revealed" with 👍
